news_summary: Today’s news highlights global progress in promoting justice and accountability, reinforcing commitment to human rights and the rule of law on International Justice Day.
news_summary_fr: À l'occasion de la Journée internationale de la justice, l'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière les progrès réalisés à l'échelle mondiale en matière de promotion de la justice et de la responsabilité, ainsi que le renforcement de l'engagement en faveur des droits de l'homme et de l'État de droit.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy destacan los avances a nivel mundial en la promoción de la justicia y la rendición de cuentas, lo que refuerza el compromiso con los derechos humanos y el Estado de derecho con motivo del Día Internacional de la Justicia.

articles.title: Marking International Justice Day
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Victims’ families and activists gather outside the International Criminal Court in The Hague to demand justice for the thousands of people killed during the abusive “drug war” of former Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte, February 23, 2026.
                    © 2026 Liona Li/TMHK via Nexpher Images/Sipa USA via AP Photo
          
    


  
On July 17, 1998, countries around the world adopted the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court (ICC), a day now recognized as International Justice Day.  The ICC is the court of last resort for victims seeking justice for the most serious crimes in violation of international law. However, this year victims' access to justice is under threat as the United States escalates its attack on the global rule of law. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio earlier this week announced a stepped-up campaign to "dismantle" the ICC. Behind the rhetoric, the US government wants to hand out “get-out–of–jail–free" cards to whomever it chooses.In the face of these and other attacks on justice, crucial efforts are being undertaken, often led by survivors, to protect and advance the international justice system.The ICC continues its critical work, including sending a first case from Libya to trial earlier this week. And the trial of former Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte for alleged crimes against humanity during the murderous “war on drugs” is set to start in November.ICC member countries, United Nations experts, and others have spoken out against the US government’s use of sanctions intended for human rights violators against ICC officials and those working to promote justice.A number of countries are also working to advance justice at home. In Syria, the first atrocity crimes trial within the country and the ongoing transitional justice process, with their challenges and shortcomings still to be addressed, provide hope that justice is finally within reach. In Lebanon, two members of parliament introduced a bill that would allow prosecution of serious international crimes, opening the door for long-awaited domestic investigations.In Myanmar and Sudan, survivor and civil society organizations are spearheading creative efforts to seek justice in other countries under the principle of universal jurisdiction, as there are no meaningful avenues for justice in their own countries.On International Justice Day – and every day –  governments should build on these efforts and show they stand by victims of atrocities. They should denounce attacks on the international justice system, show support for the ICC and those cooperating with it, and take concrete steps to protect those under attack.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Victims’ families and activists gather outside the International Criminal Court in The Hague to demand justice for the thousands of people killed during the abusive “drug war” of former Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte, February 23, 2026. © 2026 Liona Li/TMHK via Nexpher Images/Sipa USA via AP Photo

On July 17, 1998, countries around the world adopted the Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court (ICC), a day now recognized as International Justice Day.

The ICC is the court of last resort for victims seeking justice for the most serious crimes in violation of international law. However, this year victims' access to justice is under threat as the United States escalates its attack on the global rule of law. US Secretary of State Marco Rubio earlier this week announced a stepped-up campaign to "dismantle" the ICC. Behind the rhetoric, the US government wants to hand out “get-out–of–jail–free" cards to whomever it chooses.

In the face of these and other attacks on justice, crucial efforts are being undertaken, often led by survivors, to protect and advance the international justice system.

The ICC continues its critical work, including sending a first case from Libya to trial earlier this week. And the trial of former Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte for alleged crimes against humanity during the murderous “war on drugs” is set to start in November.

ICC member countries, United Nations experts, and others have spoken out against the US government’s use of sanctions intended for human rights violators against ICC officials and those working to promote justice.

A number of countries are also working to advance justice at home. In Syria, the first atrocity crimes trial within the country and the ongoing transitional justice process, with their challenges and shortcomings still to be addressed, provide hope that justice is finally within reach. In Lebanon, two members of parliament introduced a bill that would allow prosecution of serious international crimes, opening the door for long-awaited domestic investigations.

In Myanmar and Sudan, survivor and civil society organizations are spearheading creative efforts to seek justice in other countries under the principle of universal jurisdiction, as there are no meaningful avenues for justice in their own countries.

On International Justice Day – and every day – governments should build on these efforts and show they stand by victims of atrocities. They should denounce attacks on the international justice system, show support for the ICC and those cooperating with it, and take concrete steps to protect those under attack.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights ongoing and expanding international efforts to pursue justice for victims of serious crimes and atrocities, including trials and legislative actions in multiple countries. Despite challenges and opposition, these efforts represent meaningful progress in upholding human rights and the rule of law globally, providing hope and tangible impact for victims and societies.
articles.personality_title: International Justice Day highlights global efforts to hold criminals accountable
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – International Justice Day marks the adoption of the Rome Statute in 1998, which created the International Criminal Court (ICC). The ICC is a special court that helps victims of serious crimes like war crimes and crimes against humanity seek justice when other courts cannot.

**What happened** – On this day in 2026, the ICC continues its important work despite challenges. The court recently sent a case from Libya to trial and will soon start the trial of former Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte for crimes during his “war on drugs.” Meanwhile, some countries like Syria and Lebanon are making new laws and holding trials to address serious crimes within their own borders. Survivors and groups from Myanmar and Sudan are also finding ways to seek justice in other countries because they lack options at home.

**Impact** – These actions show that many countries and people are committed to making sure serious crimes do not go unpunished. Even though some powerful countries, like the United States, are criticizing and trying to weaken the ICC, the court and its supporters are pushing forward. New trials and laws give hope to victims and their families that justice can be reached.

**What’s next step** – The trial of Rodrigo Duterte will begin in November 2026, marking a major moment for international justice. Other countries are expected to continue building their own justice systems. International groups will likely keep defending the ICC and supporting efforts to protect victims and witnesses.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Despite challenges, International Justice Day 2026 highlights growing global actions to hold leaders and criminals accountable for serious crimes and support victims worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Journée internationale de la justice met en lumière les efforts mondiaux pour tenir les criminels responsables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Journée internationale de la justice célèbre l’adoption du Statut de Rome en 1998, qui a créé la Cour pénale internationale (CPI). La CPI est un tribunal spécial qui aide les victimes de crimes graves comme les crimes de guerre et les crimes contre l’humanité à obtenir justice lorsque d’autres tribunaux ne peuvent pas le faire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2026, la CPI poursuit son travail important malgré les défis. Le tribunal a récemment envoyé un dossier de Libye en procès et commencera bientôt le procès de l’ancien président philippin Rodrigo Duterte pour des crimes commis lors de sa « guerre contre la drogue ». Parallèlement, des pays comme la Syrie et le Liban adoptent de nouvelles lois et organisent des procès pour traiter de crimes graves sur leur territoire. Des survivants et des groupes du Myanmar et du Soudan cherchent aussi à obtenir justice dans d’autres pays faute d’options chez eux.

**Impact** – Ces actions montrent que de nombreux pays et personnes veulent s’assurer que les crimes graves ne restent pas impunis. Même si certains pays puissants, comme les États-Unis, critiquent et tentent d’affaiblir la CPI, le tribunal et ses soutiens avancent. Ces nouveaux procès et lois donnent de l’espoir aux victimes et à leurs familles.

**Prochaine étape** – Le procès de Rodrigo Duterte commencera en novembre 2026, un moment important pour la justice internationale. D’autres pays devraient continuer à renforcer leurs propres systèmes judiciaires. Les groupes internationaux continueront probablement à défendre la CPI et à soutenir la protection des victimes et des témoins.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Malgré les défis, la Journée internationale de la justice 2026 souligne les actions mondiales croissantes pour tenir les responsables de crimes graves comptables et soutenir les victimes partout dans le monde.
articles.personality_title_es: El Día Internacional de la Justicia destaca los esfuerzos mundiales para responsabilizar a los criminales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Día Internacional de la Justicia conmemora la adopción del Estatuto de Roma en 1998, que creó la Corte Penal Internacional (CPI). La CPI es un tribunal especial que ayuda a las víctimas de crímenes graves como crímenes de guerra y crímenes contra la humanidad a obtener justicia cuando otros tribunales no pueden.

**Qué pasó** – En 2026, la CPI continúa su trabajo importante a pesar de los desafíos. Recientemente envió un caso de Libia a juicio y pronto comenzará el juicio contra el expresidente filipino Rodrigo Duterte por crímenes cometidos durante su “guerra contra las drogas”. Mientras tanto, países como Siria y Líbano están creando nuevas leyes y realizando juicios para tratar crímenes graves en sus propios territorios. Sobrevivientes y grupos de Myanmar y Sudán también buscan justicia en otros países porque no tienen opciones en casa.

**Impacto** – Estas acciones muestran que muchos países y personas están comprometidos a que los crímenes graves no queden impunes. Aunque algunos países poderosos, como Estados Unidos, critican y tratan de debilitar a la CPI, la corte y sus apoyos siguen adelante. Los nuevos juicios y leyes dan esperanza a las víctimas y sus familias.

**Próximo paso** – El juicio de Rodrigo Duterte comenzará en noviembre de 2026, un momento importante para la justicia internacional. Se espera que otros países sigan fortaleciendo sus propios sistemas de justicia. Los grupos internacionales probablemente continuarán defendiendo a la CPI y apoyando la protección de víctimas y testigos.

**Resumen en una frase** – A pesar de los desafíos, el Día Internacional de la Justicia 2026 destaca las crecientes acciones globales para responsabilizar a líderes y criminales por crímenes graves y apoyar a las víctimas en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a balanced scale of justice held firmly by strong, intertwined hands emerging from a diverse crowd of abstract, faceless silhouettes standing united outside a stylized courthouse resembling the International Criminal Court, with soft natural colors emphasizing hope and solidarity in the face of adversity.
